speaker: interviewer
text: When you were taken from the transit-- I mean after [? Plashaf ?] and the transit-- did you know you were going to Czechoslovakia? Did you know where you were headed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, we probably did know. I don't remember. I guess, I believe that we did know, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you got there, what we are living conditions like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were-- originally, we were-- or initially, we were sleeping on the factory floor just with a lot of straw-- loose straw-- laying on it. Eventually, Mr. Schindler provided bunk beds, two levels, and mattresses which we filled with straw-- not much, just what we know that we had today. Some had a pillow, which was filled with straw. And a grey blanket, like an army blanket. This, everybody got that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the factory? The name of the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no name that I know of. I don't know of any specific name for the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well when you call it Brunnlitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Brunnlitz is the town-- the name in which, or next to, which this was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the SS would guard the factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. But they would guard the perimeter of the camp over which we had built the electric fence.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your days like? Were you up-- did they wake you early in the morning? How did the schedule go?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were awaken about 6 o'clock. We worked-- we were awake at 6 o'clock. We were given coffee, which was not even coffee. It was what they used to call ersatz cafe. Roasted wheat or some kind of a grain, but it was black. The color was quite good. The taste was horrible. And a piece of bread, a slice of bread. At lunch time, we were given a soup. Usually nothing more but the soup. In the evening, we got another piece of bread. I was a peculiar person. I could not eat-- the soup had some flakes of meat, usually. Here the soup was much better than concentration camps. But I couldn't eat it because I knew that this is horse meat. So I had-- the soup was delivered in cans. I mean, you had your own can, and you come up, and you get the soup. And I fished out meat, I traded it for a piece of bread. So this was my [INAUDIBLE] grounds. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Eventually, after the fence was built, I was moved into the factory. I was still an electrician. I became in charge of one shift. There were two or three shifts of working. One shift in charge of electric ovens, which were annealing in material from which we were the Schindler's camp was to produce ammunition. Annealing means softening of the metal so it can be drawn in large presses. The product which we were supposed to be making-- which we were making- were tips of anti-armor shells. They consisted of a-- they were shaped-- a conical shape about 12 inches or 14 inches long to a sharp point. And at the opposite end, it had a female thread which was to mate with another part made in some other place. We were not to make up the other part. We were only to ship these parts which we were manufacturing to the factory somewhere else, which would assemble them. As I was told later, Schindler was changing the drawings to such that none of these parts had correct threads to mate with the parts when they arrived in the other end. And supposedly, all these months, we were producing worthless merchandise. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: During the time-- approximately December 1944, possibly early January 1945-- I was approached by one of the Jewish prisoners asking was I interested in doing something which will be dangerous, but might save our lives? I said, describe it to me. Maybe I'm interested.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he described it to me. We are forming an underground group because we believe that the time will come-- and we hear from other sources that such time has come other places already-- that they would kill us regardless of what Schindler would do. Therefore we must protect ourselves. We have to try to get weapons. We will try to be contact without outside undergrounds and see whether we can save ourselves that way. And I said yes, I'm interested. I was assigned another partner, and the two of us were one cell of the group. We knew nobody else. We just knew each other. And we knew where to find instructions. Instructions were always in writing. And they were to be placed in a dark place. And every so often, we were to go and recover them, read them, and follow them. The friend who was my partner is still alive, lives in New York. I will not mention his name. Maybe he mentioned mine, because he has already gone through it. I don't know. We were in touch-- got in touch with the Czechoslovakian underground, which told us that they will need at least six hours to mobilize in case the general wants to kill us, so they can help us and save us. We must be able to withstand the six hours. And the only way we can withstand six hours is having weapons. They got in contact with Schindler, and they somehow made a deal with Schindler that he will deliver us weapons. One time, I recovered a sheet of paper in the place, which some instructions were hidden. That on this and this night, at 12:30 in the morning, I am to knock on Mr. Schindler's door, and I will receive a weapon. I wrapped myself in that grey blanket. I went through the factory. The factory was working, because there was a night shift. I knew where Schindler's office is. I knocked on Schindler's door. It was exactly 12:30.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, wir ist das? Who is that? And I told him Joseph. He opened the door only about six inches and gave me a rifle and a box of ammunition. I put it under my coat and carried it back to my bed. Within five minutes, somebody came-- one of the underground groups-- and took it away from me. All these-- several weapons were delivered that way, to different people, including a machine gun-- a small machine gun-- and all these things were hidden in the kitchen.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have to explain the workings of the concentration camp for you to understand why in the kitchen. Every morning and every evening we were called into the plaza and we were counted. But the people who worked in the kitchen were not counted in the plaza. They were counted in the kitchen. And from previous experiences, we knew that if any action is to be taken, the people in the kitchen would be called-- be ordered to come to the plaza. Therefore we knew that the kitchen was safe at least to the point when which they wish to take some action against us. The kitchen had, if I remember correctly, six kettles in which the soups and things were cooked. One of the kettles was always disabled and supposedly being always repaired. Under that kettle the ammunition was stored, and the ammunition and the weapons are stored. The intention was if the order should come, that the kitchen is to report-- they will report to the plaza where they will be counted, they would not go. They would send out the signal. They-- I didn't know how many at that time-- the ones who are assigned to this underground would come to the kitchen, grab the weapons, and try to defend ourselves to last for six hours until [INAUDIBLE] the underground, hoping that they will come to our help. It didn't come to that because the Germans just capitulated on the 8th of May, and we did not need to use these weapons, at least not for that purpose. We did use them. But this is a story in its own way. But I wish to retract-- [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] Now I'm getting a little bit confused myself. You have to retract, to a point, the Germans-- the Germans evacuated many places in the East. And Schindler travelled there, and somehow he got contacts. He was allowed to take all of the goods out of these places where they evacuated which the Germans themselves-- the armies-- could not take. He brought it all into the camp, and he was distributing tobacco and cookies and cakes and everything else to the people. All he organized in that way. He also convinced the Germans to bring to his factory many truckloads of fabrics from somewhere. And these were fabrics-- shirt materials, slacks materials, coat materials, heavy, light. My God, this was a factory filled to about 10 or 15 feet high. You had to climb and walk all over that in order to see it all. And eventually, this material was distributed among all the people at Schindler's, and everybody took his package with him. I, being one of those, eventually who found out 28 or 30 underground people were given special privileges. We took much more. Which I eventually sold in Krakow after we arrived there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- I mean, so he was considered a hero to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who?

speaker: interviewer
text: Schindler. Throughout--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We don't know hero, not hero. He was considered a savior. Hero? Maybe he was a hero.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well I guess my question was--

speaker: interviewee
text: In my opinion, I agree with Mrs. Schindler's opinion. In truth, he saved my life. He was crazy. You can't imagine what he did. How many times he risked his own life to accomplish it. And only a person who is not quite right in his mind would do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you think that's what motivated him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. No one knows who motivated him. This is Mrs. Schindler's opinion.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have contact with his wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only indirectly. I had no contact with his wife one on one. No, no. Just to go back to Schindler's camp-- you see, at Schindler's camp, nobody was killed during the time. There were-- to the best of my knowledge, two people died, one girl of some kind of an infectious disease, for which Schindler had provided a separate room for her and food and everything else. And the doctor-- we had a couple doctors there, Jewish doctors-- who had treated her, but they couldn't save her. And one older man, Mr. [? Garder. ?] He was probably 75, 80 years old. He died a natural death. There's one more instant, which I think just like the situation with the weapons did not come up in any writings about Schindler's camp, there's one more thing which came up in a very short way. Sometimes during, I believe, March 1945, Schindler was informed that on a railroad sighting outside his camp-- several kilometers outside-- there were three railroad cars full of Jewish prisoners abandoned by the SS. And it was winter. They were frozen. He took a couple trucks and several people with him-- and I was not one of them-- and went there and opened the railroad trucks and pulled out many, many dead people. But if I remember correctly, maybe 50 of them were still alive. He brought them back into the Schindler's camp. He set them up in an empty factory building. Mrs. Schindler and him fed them all the goodies which the Germans couldn't even get, milk, cereals, whatever, and brought, to the best of my knowledge, all of them back to health. They were pretty much half dead when they arrived.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you know that happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: How do you know that it happened? I was there. I saw them coming in. I talked to them after they were placed in the empty factory. I talked to them. Eventually, they were incorporated into the working force, but for a very short time because it was almost liberation time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else about Schindler's camp that you can recall that you want to share with us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, about three days before the actual capitulation of Germany, Schindler called on the electrical department to take large stickers from his office, hang them in the factory, and from his beautiful Blaupunkt stereo-- maybe not stereo, but radio-- beam BBC News from England onto the factory so that we would be aware of what is going on. The SS was still in power. But he, risking, in my opinion, his life, allowed this to happen. And wires were wheeled out. That's when our guy was involved in it, to a small degree. Speakers were placed, and we heard from London, from BBC-- in Polish, because we didn't speak English-- in Polish the news about German capitulation. The day before the Germans actually capitulated, with the SS still being in the camp, Schindler had what is known now as his famous speech. The speech was a little bit different than is being publicized now. And I don't remember it. I don't have it written down anywhere. However it started with 'in a day or two, the gates of the camp will open. You will become free. However, you will start looking for your families, and you won't find them. They will be all dead.' [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] 'In your future lives, remember that above all--' I'm sorry. [DRINKS] Not all Germans were guilty. That's all. I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the SS walking around throughout the factory during the days, or were they more outside the fence?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the SS-- throughout the day, would they be roaming the factory, watching what was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I understand that Schindler has made some kind of deal with them that they would not be. But from time to time the German officers-- especially the SS officers, and maybe even non-commissioned officers, not the guards-- were coming, walking through the factory. You see, Schindler himself had developed a certain technique. The factory consisted of many machines, each one occupied by one Jewish prisoner doing a certain type of work. Schindler used to come in the morning and several times during the day, and walk through the factories. He lit a cigarette and put it on the machine and walked. On every machine there was a cigarette, a lit cigarette, so you could smoke. He couldn't give you the cigarette because he was afraid that somebody may look at it. And a bit later, when he started recovering items from the areas the Germans were evacuating, he did bring us cigarettes, tobacco-- my God, many, many things. I can even-- I don't even remember what was what. But every so often, he used different excuses. Tomorrow is my wife's birthday. I'm going to give the Jews bread. We got an extra bread. He always used some gimmicks to help the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were in his camp at one time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, before they brought in the 50 people from the frozen train loads, which were-- the train came from [? Golashov-- ?] we were, I think, about 800 men and about 300 some women. But 1,100, give or take a few, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: After Schindler's speech, what happens next? When are you liberated? Are you liberated a few days later?

speaker: interviewee
text: The following day. After Schindler's speech, Schindler loaded-- or they were already being loaded-- two trucks and his car-- Horch, it's a very large limousine. And, to make the story short, Schindler and the trucks left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Make the story long. Is there more? I can't make the story long because it is not representative by the popular knowledge of it as some people know or have seen it over there, without referring to 'Schindler's List.' It was not exactly that way. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The following day, the following morning, we woke up. We found that the gates are open. The Germans are gone. The weapons the Germans pulled out locks from their rifles and dropped them on the ground. And whether they took the rifles with them or not, they were useless without the locks. And we were on our own. Let me ask one last question about Schindler. As he was leaving, do you remember what the mood was like? What the feeling of the people were as he was leaving?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were still under the impression of his speech from the day before. He said the gates will open and you'll be free. And this is what we knew. This was just-- we trusted the man. We believed that he said what he meant and it was that way. The following morning, the day, the gates were opened. The Germans were gone. We took out the weapons from the kitchen. And now I have to tell you a story about what happened right after that. Within our people-- the 28, 30 underground-- manned the guardhouses. Within a few hours after the Germans left, a motorcycle with a side wagon came up with the SS, and they wanted to speak to Mr. Schindler. And our guards in the boots pointed their guns at them and said Mr. Schindler is gone. You cannot come in. But we can't go anywhere. We need gas. We gave them gas. Schindler left warehouses. We gave them gasoline. And they wanted plans of the factory, of what Schindler was doing. But we didn't give it to them and they just turned around and went away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this the first time you knew who else was in the underground?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you surprised at who was involved?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes and no. As a matter of fact, I was surprised that no one else except my friend and I were people from Krakow. The other people were from who knows where. And I don't know them. I never knew them then. And I don't know them now. I never was in contact with them after I left Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you don't even know why they chose you.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I guess observation or whatever, behavior, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: The other thing I didn't understand, going back, how are they able to make contact with the Czechoslovakian underground?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were Czechoslovakian people, specialists, working in the camp, coming and going. And we used them as-- what do you call them-- communication links.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they would come into the camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were coming into the camp. No, they were not necessarily part of the underground. We didn't know that. Maybe they were, maybe they were not.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were just Czech people who came-- specialists in whatever we needed-- and they-- but there were several of them, five, seven, nine, whatever-- and they used to go home for the evening then come back in the morning. They had a pass. They had a pass. Somebody must have approached them. It wasn't I, and I wasn't even-- no, I didn't know about it. And it was done in pairs so, case somebody gets caught, he doesn't know who else there is is in it. And eventually, we got the a response from the Czechoslovakian underground. Which, as I mentioned before, that if we can hold them up for six hours, they will be able to help. This was how much time they need to mobilize. So now you're in the booth with the whole group of underground, holding your weapons. How many weapons in all did you end up with?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had six rifles, a machine gun, and several side arms, revolvers. I was given the revolver because I got the stupid job. And this is something which has to be started again, with a different end. But the people who were frozen in the train arrived, with them arrived another prisoner what was known as [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] which is professional criminal, who was a German, not a Jew, who was a kapo in the camp from which these Jews came. And he was at Camp Schindler. And he also was revived. And he was always OK. He became very strong. And he tried to carry his weight. But we were in charge. He couldn't carry his weight. He was no longer a kapo. But we interviewed-- not we, I say we, I mean some of the 28, not I-- and found out how many people he killed in the camp he came from. What they used to do is, they used to get-- a kapo used to get an assignment of, let's say, so many breads. And the fewer breads you give out, the more he had for himself. If you killed three people today, you got three portions of bread extra. It was his. This is why they killed people. Eventually I got the responsibility of guarding him till some authority would arrive and decide what to do with him. To us, he was a criminal. When the Russian came in-- not the first Russian, but eventually a few days later came a Russian colonel-- who was actually Jewish, spoke Yiddish, spoke Yiddish, very few of us spoke Russian-- and we told him the story about who the man is. And this colonel told us look, in a few days in our administration, a group will come in and will register you. Whoever will be in camp will be registered. And whoever will not be here anymore, who will die, won't be registered. So you know what they're going to do with him. And they hung him. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Before that administration came they hung him, right in the factory. The people from [? Golashov ?] who came frozen in the car with him, they hung him. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now, we were liberated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's wait and go to the next tape. We're almost at the very end of this tape. So let's switch

speaker: interviewee
text: How many tapes you want?
